zh.lifeSummary: 當Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare於大約1114年出生在英國林可爾史爾馬可特 迪平時，她的父親Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne23歲，母親Adeline de Rollos23歲。她於1144年與Hugh Wake在英國林可爾史爾馬可特 迪平結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世於1168年在英國林可爾史爾尼享年55歲，埋葬在英國坎布利其爾尼。

zh-hans.Deeping, Lincolnshire, England: 英国林可爾史爾馬可特 迪平

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare于大约1114年出生在英国林可爾史爾馬可特 迪平时，她的父亲Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne23岁，母亲Adeline de Rollos23岁。她于1144年与Hugh Wake在英国林可爾史爾馬可特 迪平结婚。他们至少是2个儿子和1个女儿的父母。她去世于1168年在英国林可爾史爾尼享年55岁，埋葬在英国坎布利其爾尼。

nameMeanings.name: fitz
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (from Latin filius), used originally to distinguish a son from a father bearing the same personal name and then conventionalized as a hereditary surname, or short for a surname of which fiz ‘son’ was the first element, for example Fitzhenry and Fitzroy .
Altered form of English   Fitch  .
German: from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Vinzenz (see Vincent ) or Vitus (see Vito , compare Veit ). Compare also Fietz .

variants.name: Fitzgerald
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Eamon, Liam, Aidan, Bridgid, Caitlin, Donal, Fitz, Fitzgerald, Ciaran, Colum, Dermot.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Gerald (see Gerald ), ‘son of Gerald’. This is a very common Anglo-Norman name in Ireland, where it has been Gaelicized as Mac Gearailt. Irish bearers of the name claim descent from Gerald Fitzwalter de Windsor, constable of Pembroke Castle in the early 12th century, whose grandson Raymond (or Redmond) ‘le Gros’ FitzGerald (died after 1185) led the invasion of Ireland on behalf of Strongbow in 1170. A branch of the family held the title Knight of Glin from at least 1299–2011.

variants.name: Fitzwater
meanings: Irish: altered form of Fitzwalter, a patronymic from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Walter , meaning ‘son of Walter’.

History: This is the name of a family of Norman origin, descended from Godfrey, Count of Brionne (died c. 1010). Godfrey's grandson, Richard FitzGilbert, accompanied William the Conqueror and was made Lord of Clare. His great-grandson was Robert FitzWalter, one of the barons who guaranteed the Magna Carta in 1215.

variants.name: Fitzherbert
meanings: English and Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Herbert (see Herbert ), ‘son of Herbert’.

variants.name: Fitzhenry
meanings: Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Henry (see Henry ), ‘son of Henry’. This surname is common in Ireland (mainly Connacht), where it has been Gaelicized as Mac Éinrí. In at least some cases, it seems to have been adopted as a surname indicating the illegitimate offspring of a man called Henry.

variants.name: Fitzsimmons
meanings: Irish and English: variant of Fitzsimon, with post-medieval excrescent -s, from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + either of the personal names Simon or Sigmund (see Simon ), ‘son of Simon or Sigmund’. The name is also found in the Gaelicized form Mac Síomóin.

variants.name: Fitzgibbon
meanings: Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Gibbon, a Norman French pet form of Gilbert ; ‘son of Gibbon’. This name has long been a widespread and important surname in Ireland, and Gaelicized as Mac Giobúin. Sir Gilbert (Gibbon) FitzJohn, illegitimate son of John FitzGerald, 1st Baron Desmond (died 1261), was the ancestor of a line of Anglo-Norman aristocracy in Ireland, who acquired the surname Fitzgibbon with reference to him. Gilbert and his Fitzgibbon descendants were known as ‘the White Knights’ on account of their fair complexion.

variants.name: Fitzharris
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Aileen.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Harry ‘son of Harry’. MacLysaght describes this as the name of a distinguished County Wexford family, also known as Fitzhenry .

variants.name: Fitzmaurice
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Aidan, Ciaran, Colm.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Maurice (see Morris 1), ‘son of Maurice’. This surname is common in Ireland, where it has also been Gaelicized as Mac Muiris. The Irish family of Fitzmaurice share a common Norman ancestry with the Fitzgerald family. They can be traced to Thomas Fitzmaurice, whose brother Gerald was the ancestor of the Irish Fitzgerald dynasty. Their father was Maurice Fitzgerald, a Norman baron (died 1176).

variants.name: Fitzhugh
meanings: English (Northamptonshire): from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Hugh (see Hugh ), ‘son of Hugh’.

History: William Fitzhugh (1651–1701), from Bedford, England, emigrated to VA c. 1670 and established himself on the Potomac River in what was then Stafford County, VA, as a planter and exporter. He also practiced law, was a member of the Virginia House of Burgesses, and served in 1687 as lieutenant colonel of the county militia.

variants.name: Fitzroy
meanings: English: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (from Latin filius), used originally to distinguish a son from a father bearing the same personal name and then conventionalized as a hereditary surname, or short for a surname of which fiz ‘son’ was the first element, for example Fitzhenry and Fitzroy .
Altered form of English   Fitch  .
German: from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Vinzenz (see Vincent ) or Vitus (see Vito , compare Veit ). Compare also Fietz .

nameMeanings.name: Baldwin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and North German: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements bald ‘bold, brave’ + wine ‘friend’, which was extremely popular among the Normans and in Flanders in the early Middle Ages. It was the personal name of the Crusader who in 1100 became the first Christian king of Jerusalem, and of four more Crusader kings of Jerusalem. It was also borne by Baldwin, Count of Flanders (1172–1205), leader of the Fourth Crusade, who became first Latin Emperor of Constantinople (1204). In North America, this surname has absorbed Dutch forms such as Boudewijn.
Irish: surname adopted in Donegal by bearers of the Gaelic surname Ó Maolagáin (see Milligan ), due to association of Gaelic maol ‘bald, hairless’ with English bald.
History: A John Baldwin from Buckinghamshire, England, arrived in the US in 1638 and settled in Milford, CT.

variants.name: Milligan
meanings: Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Maolagáin ‘descendant of Maoileagán’, a personal name from a double diminutive of maol meaning ‘bald’ or ‘tonsured’.

variants.name: Baldovino
meanings: Hispanic (mainly Philippines and Colombia) and Italian: from the personal name Baldovino, from ancient Germanic Baldowin (see Baldwin ).

variants.name: Bellmann
meanings: North German:
habitational name for someone from Belle in Westphalia, Bell in the Rhineland, or Bellen near Bremen.
in Saxony, from a pet form of the ancient Germanic personal name   Baldwin  .

variants.name: Bais
meanings: Dutch (mainly North Holland): patronymic from the personal name Baije, which is a derivative of an ancient Germanic name beginning with the element bern- ‘bear’, or possibly a variant of Boije, which may be a derivative of Boidin and thus a variant of Baldwin .
Italian (Friuli Venezia Giulia and Trentino-South Tyrol): nickname derived from German weiss ‘white’.

Americanized form of Croatian and Slovenian Bajs: nickname from dialect bajs ‘bass’ (the musical instrument), figuratively ‘fatty, potbelly’. The surname in the form Bais is found in Croatia, too, where it is a less common variant.

variants.name: Baudoin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Camille, Etienne, Adrien, Alcide, Andre, Firmin, Francoise, Jean-Yves, Laurent, Minos, Sylvain.
French: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements bald ‘bold, brave’ + win ‘friend’ (see Baldwin ). Compare Boudoin 1 and Bowdoin .
Altered form of French   Beaudoin  , a cognate (variant) of 1 above.

variants.name: Bald
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Inge, Karl-Heinz, Konrad, Kurt, Otto.
German: from the ancient Germanic personal name Baldo, or a short form of various compound names such as Baldwin , formed with the first element bald ‘bold, strong’.

Scottish and English: from the Norman personal name Bald(e), Baud(e), occasionally Bold, a short form of the common ancient Germanic personal names Baldwin or Baldric, or possibly a survival of Old English Beald (compare Balding ); the relevant element in each of these is b(e)ald ‘brave’. As a hereditary surname it seems to have died out in England but survives in Scotland.

variants.name: Balkin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Emanuel, Herschel.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): from the Yiddish female personal name Beyle meaning ‘beautiful’ (related to French belle).

English (of Norman origin): perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English personal name Baldekin, Baudekin, an Anglo-Norman borrowing of Middle Dutch Baudequin, a Flemish pet form of Baldwin . However, a clear link between Middle English Baudekin and post-medieval Balkin has not been established, and the modern name may have a different source, a variant of Balcom perhaps, by migration.

variants.name: Bawden
meanings: English: from a late variant of the Norman personal name   Baldwin   or a variant of   Bowden  .

variants.name: Baldon
meanings: English: habitational name from either Marsh Baldon or Toot Baldon in Oxfordshire, from the Old English personal name Bealda + dūn ‘hill’, or a variant of Baldwin . Occasionally the name may be from Baildon in Yorkshire (named with Old English bēgel ‘bend’ + dūn ‘hill’; recorded as Baldon in the 16th century), or from Ballidon in Derbyshire (named with Old English belg ‘bag, sack’ + denu ‘valley’).
English: variant of   Boldon  .
Italian (Veneto): from an augmentative of the personal name   Baldo  .

variants.name: Beaudoin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Marcel, Andre, Serge, Jacques, Gilles, Laurent, Normand, Pierre, Alcide, Lucien, Raoul.

French: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements bald ‘bold, brave’ + win ‘friend’ (see Baldwin ). Compare Baudoin , Baudouin , Beaudion , Boadway , Boardway , Bodeau , Bodoh , Bodway , Boudway , and Budway .

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Clare
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Irish (Clare, Wicklow, and Wexford): habitational name from Clare in Suffolk (an eastern county of England), the center of a major Norman baronial estate since the Norman Conquest. The English placename probably originated as a Celtic river name meaning ‘bright, gentle, or warm’. The surname is not from the Irish county name Clare, which is either from the surname or from a place called with Gaelic clár ‘plank bridge’. Another Norman surname in the southeast of Ireland was de Clere, from Clères in Seine-Maritime (France), named from the local river.

English and French: from the Middle English, Old French female personal name Cla(i)re (from Latin Clara, from clarus ‘clear, bright, famous’) which achieved some popularity, greater elsewhere in Europe than in England, through the fame of Saint Clare of Assisi (see Chiara ). The English surname seems to have been interchangeable with Clear 1.

English: occupational name from an agent derivative of Middle English cley ‘clay’. The work of medieval clayers involved plastering a framework of interwoven twigs with mud to produce wattle-and-daub work.

variants.name: Claire
meanings: Irish: variant of   Clare  .
Variant of   Clair  , an altered form of German   Klare  .
French: variant of   Clair  .

variants.name: Chiara
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Marco, Amedeo, Angelo, Gino, Rocco.

Italian: from the female personal name Chiara or Clara, a name popularized in the medieval period by the cult of Saint Clare of Assisi (c. 1193–1253); she was an associate of Saint Francis of Assisi and founded the order of nuns known as the Poor Clares. See also Chiaro .

variants.name: Claar
meanings: German: variant of Klaar (and, in North America, an altered form of this), a nickname from Middle High German, Middle Low German klar ‘pure, pretty, sensible’, or a habitational name from a minor place so named near Braunschweig.
Possibly also an Americanized form of Dutch Klaar: from a vernacular form of the female personal name Clara (see Clare 2).

variants.name: Clarkin
meanings: English (Lancashire and Cheshire): from a pet form of the personal name Clare (compare Clare and Clear ), which is both a masculine and a female personal name in Middle English.
Irish (Monaghan): variant of   Clerkin  .

variants.name: Clear
meanings: English (southeastern England, of Norman origin): from Clere, the French popular form of the personal name Clare 2, and possibly interchangeable with it or confused with it.

English (southeastern): probably a habitational name from clere, a component of several placenames in north Hampshire (Highclere, Burghclere, Kingsclere). This is probably from a Celtic stream name meaning ‘bright’ (cognate with Latin clarus ‘clear, bright’).
Americanized form (translation into English) of German   Klar   1.

nameMeanings.name: Emma
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Old French name, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, originally a short form of compound names such as Ermintrude containing the word erm(en), irm(en) ‘entire’. It was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain, but its popularity in medieval England was greatly enhanced by the fact that it had been borne by the mother of Edward the Confessor, herself a Norman. In modern times, it was only in moderate use early in the 20th century but rose sharply in favour in the 1970s and has since remained perennially popular.

en.lifeSummary: When Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare was born about 1114, in Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England, her father, Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne, was 23 and her mother, Adeline de Rollos, was 23. She married Hugh Wake in 1144, in Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 1 daughter. She died in 1168, in Thorney, Lincolnshire, England, at the age of 55, and was buried in Thorney, Cambridgeshire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare est née environ 1114, à Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Angleterre, son père, Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne, avait 23 ans et sa mère, Adeline de Rollos, avait 23 ans. Elle a épousé Hugh Wake en 1144, à Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 1 fille. Elle est décédée en 1168, à Thorney, Lincolnshire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 55 ans, et a été enterrée à Thorney, Cambridgeshire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare um 1114 in Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England geboren wurde, war ihr Vater Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne 23 und ihre Mutter Adeline de Rollos 23. Sie hat 1144 in Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England Hugh Wake geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 2 Söhne und 1 Tochter. Sie starb 1168 im Alter von 55 Jahren in Thorney, Lincolnshire, England und wurde in Thorney, Cambridgeshire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare è nata il circa 1114 nel Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England, sua padre, Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne, aveva 23 anni e sua madre, Adeline de Rollos, aveva 23 anni. Lei sposato Hugh Wake nel 1144, nel Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, England. Erano genitori di almeno 2 figli e 1 figlia. Morì nel 1168, nel Thorney, Lincolnshire, England, all'età di 55 anni, e fu sepolta a Thorney, Cambridgeshire, England.

ja.lifeSummary: Emma fitz Baldwin de Clareが約1114年にイギリスリンコルンシュアイアマーケト ディーパングで生まれた時，父Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourneは23歳，母Adeline de Rollosは23歳であった。1144年にイギリスリンコルンシュアイアマーケト ディーパングでHugh Wakeと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子と1人の娘の親であった。彼女は1168年にイギリスリンコルンシュアイアソルニーで55歳で死去し，イギリスカンブリジェシュアイアソルニーにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare(이)가 대략 1114년 영국 링코실르 마크트 디핀그에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne(은)는 23세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Adeline de Rollos(은)는 23세였다. 그녀는 1144년 영국 링코실르 마크트 디핀그에서 Hugh Wake(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1168년 영국 링코실르 토른이에서 55세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 캄브리지실르 토른이에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare nasceu aproximadamente 1114, em Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne, tinha 23 anos e sua mãe, Adeline de Rollos, tinha 23 anos. Ela casou-se com Hugh Wake em 1144, em Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra. Eles tiveram pelo menos 2 filhos e 1 filha. Ela faleceu em 1168, em Thorney, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, com 55 anos, e foi sepultada em Thorney, Cambridgeshire, Inglaterra.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare родилась около 1114 г. в местности маркет дипинг, линколншир, Англия, её отцу по имени Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne было 23 года, а матери, которую звали Adeline de Rollos, 23. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Hugh Wake в 1144 г. в местности маркет дипинг, линколншир, Англия. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 1 дочь. Она умерла в 1168 г. в местности таурни, линколншир, Англия в возрасте 55 лет и была похоронена в местности таурни, камбриджешир, Англия.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Emma fitz Baldwin de Clare nació aproximadamente 1114, en Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, su papá, Baldwin Fitz Gilbert de Clare lord of Bourne, tenía 23 años y su mamá, Adeline de Rollos, tenía 23 años. Se casó con Hugh Wake en 1144, en Market Deeping, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra. Fueron padres de por lo menos 2 hijos y 1 hija. Murió en 1168, en Thorney, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, a la edad de 55 años, y fue sepultada en Thorney, Cambridgeshire, Inglaterra.
